actions.thought: In a heads-up situation, while the button (SB) should play a wide range of hands, 64o is extremely weak with poor playability and low equity (around 38% against a random hand). In the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to fold the bottom 20-25% of hands rather than entering a pot with a hand that rarely makes a strong post-flop holding.

In Heads-Up play, any pocket pair is a playable hand from the Button. While 22 is the lowest pair and vulnerable to overcards, it still has decent equity against a random hand. Limping invites the Big Blind to see a free flop with 100% of their range, often allowing them to realize equity with hands like T5o. Raising to 200 (min-raise) puts pressure on the opponent, potentially taking the pot down immediately or allowing me to play in position with the initiative. If 3-bet, I can easily fold given the low value of the pair.

I have Ace-Jack of hearts, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Pro has raised from the button to 200. Since I am in the big blind with a strong hand, I should 3-bet for value and to take the initiative. A raise to 700 is a standard size to put pressure on the button's opening range while building a pot for my strong holding.

I hold Pocket Deuces (22), the weakest pocket pair. While playable as an opener, facing a large 3-bet (to 700) significantly reduces the hand's value. 22 plays very poorly post-flop unless a set is hit immediately (roughly 12% chance), and even then, extracting value can be difficult. The opponent's 3-bet sizing (3.5x) is strong, and I am not getting the correct immediate odds to set-mine profitably given the likelihood of facing further aggression on high-card flops. As planned in my pre-flop strategy, I will fold to the 3-bet to preserve my stack.

Holding J5o on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. While I have positional advantage post-flop, J5o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well against a professional opponent's defending range. Given it is early in the match (Hand 3 of 150), I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for better spots to utilize my position rather than bloating a pot with a sub-optimal holding.

In Heads-Up play, JTo is a strong starting hand (well above average) and I have the advantage of position (Button). While the opponent showed aggression with a 3-bet in Hand #2, I cannot let that deter standard value opens. A min-raise to 200 is the standard play to build the pot while in position and potentially take down the blinds immediately. If called, I have a playable hand that connects well with high cards.

A8o is a strong hand in a heads-up situation, well ahead of a typical button opening range. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 100 to win a pot of 300), calling is the standard play here to see a flop. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure, calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to play post-flop with a hand that has good high-card value.

On a board of 7d-9h-5h, I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) and one overcard. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the initial aggressor. This board is quite coordinated and could hit a lot of hands in both ranges, but checking allows me to see how Pro reacts before committing more chips with just Ace-high.

I hold JTo on a 7d-9h-5h flop. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) and two overcards. The board texture is wet and connects well with the Big Blind's defensive range (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). As the pre-flop raiser, while I have a range advantage in general, this specific board favors the caller slightly. Betting runs the risk of getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a Jack, Ten, or Eight on the turn, and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand that has showdown value (Jack-high).

The turn Jack of Clubs doesn't improve my Ace-high hand, though I still maintain an inside straight draw (6). Since Pro checked back on the flop, they likely have a marginal hand or missed entirely. However, the Jack is a card that could reasonably connect with a button opening range. Checking here is the best play to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, or decide based on Pro's next move. I have some showdown value with Ace-high if the hand stays small.

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs improves my hand to Top Pair with a Ten kicker, plus I retain my gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The board texture is [7d 9h 5h Jc]. The opponent checked to me again. Since I checked back the flop, my range looks capped or weak to the opponent, which might induce light calls. There are many draws available (hearts, straight draws with 6, 8, 10) that I need to charge, and I can get value from worse pairs like a 9, 7, or pocket pairs. Giving a free card here is dangerous due to the wet board. I will bet half the pot (200) to extract value and protect my hand.

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs is added to a coordinated board of 7d-9h-5h. After we both checked the flop, Pro has bet 200 into a 400 pot. I am holding Ace-high (Ac8h), which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to make a 5-6-7-8-9 straight) and one overcard to the board. In a heads-up match, Ace-high still retains significant showdown value against many of the Button's air or missed draw hands. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800), I have enough equity and implied odds when I hit my straight to justify a call. Folding here would be too weak against a standard delayed continuation bet, and raising would turn a hand with decent showdown value into a pure bluff.

The river 9s pairs the board. My Ace-high (A8) has some showdown value against missed draws like 6-4 or lower heart draws, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if the action continues aggressively. Having called the turn bet, checking the river is the most prudent play to see if Pro checks behind with a marginal hand or a missed draw. I do not want to bet and turn my hand into a bluff when I might win at showdown, nor do I want to build a pot with only Ace-high on this board.

On the river, the board is [7d 9h 5h Jc 9s]. The pot is 800. I hold [Js Td] for Top Pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker, but the board has paired Nines, which counterfeits lower two pairs but makes Trips for any 9x in the opponent's range. The opponent checked to me. 

The opponent's range for check-calling the turn includes 9x (now Trips), straight draws (some made, some missed), flush draws (missed), and weak pairs like 7x or 5x. 

If I bet:
1. Better hands (9x, straights like T8/86) will call or check-raise.
2. Worse hands (7x, 5x, missed draws) will likely fold. It is unlikely a 7 or 5 calls a river bet after the board pairs the 9.

Since a bet is unlikely to get called by worse and only builds the pot against hands that beat me, this is a clear spot to check back and take showdown value. I beat all missed draws and lower pairs.

AQo is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button (Small Blind), I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 6d. The opponent has raised to 300 (3x BB). While heads-up requires defending wide, 86o is a weak hand, and defending against a 3x open out of position is difficult. The pot odds are 33%, but the realization of equity with this hand OOP is low. Folding is the prudent long-term play against this sizing.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 8c 4h, which is a very weak offsuit hand (84o). In heads-up play, while ranges are wider, this hand falls into the bottom percentage of hands usually folded pre-flop. My opponent has shown willingness to 3-bet against my opens (Hand #2) and defend (Hand #4), so stealing with trash is risky. There is no reason to inflate the pot or call out of position post-flop with this holding. Folding is the standard play.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win it pre-flop.

I hold AJo in the Big Blind, facing a 3bb open from the Button (Flash). In Heads-Up play, AJo is a premium hand that dominates a significant portion of the opponent's opening range (worse Ax, Kx, Qx, connectors). While a 3-bet for value is a viable option, calling is often preferred to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot while playing out of position with deep stacks (100bb). Calling allows me to realize equity without isolating myself against the top of their range or facing a difficult 4-bet decision.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit on a Queen-Ten-Seven rainbow board. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a King) and one overcard (the Ace). As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture (Q-T-7) interacts well with the Button's opening range, so a donk bet is unlikely to accomplish much other than folding out bluffs or building a pot when behind. I will check and evaluate my opponent's action.

The flop of 7c-Td-Qh is quite coordinated and likely connects with a standard big blind defending range. I have Ace-high with no immediate draws. By checking behind, I control the pot size and get a free turn card to see if I improve or if the board texture changes in my favor, while also keeping the pot small for showdown value.

On the turn, the board is [7c Td Qh 2h]. The pot is 600. My opponent checked back the flop, which typically indicates a medium strength hand (like middle pair) or a complete miss giving up. The 2h is a brick that introduces a backdoor flush draw but doesn't change the board texture significantly. With Ace-Jack high, I have showdown value against their air (missed connectors, worse high cards) and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (worse Ax, Kx) and isolate me against pairs that will call. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit my straight or Ace on the river, or get to showdown cheaply. I will check to the raiser.

I have Ace-high on a board that hasn't improved my hand. The opponent has checked both the flop and the turn, which may indicate weakness or a marginal hand. By checking back, I control the pot size and realize my equity to the river for free. Ace-high retains some showdown value in heads-up play, and I prefer to keep the pot small given the current texture.

I hold Ace-Jack high on a board of [7c Td Qh 2h 6d]. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and turn. This passive line suggests the opponent likely holds a marginal hand (weak pair) or missed draws/high cards. My Ace-high has showdown value against their missed draws and worse high cards (like Kx or worse Ax). Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and would only isolate me against better hands (pairs). Checking gives the best opportunity to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff from air, though I will likely fold to a bet given the board texture favors their range having some piece (like a pair of 7s or Ts) that checked back.

On the river, the six gives me a bottom pair. The board has several overcards (7, T, Q), but my opponent has checked through every street after calling pre-flop. This passive line suggests they likely have a weak hand or a missed draw. While I might be able to extract a small amount of value from Ace-high, checking back is the safest play to guarantee winning the pot with my pair of sixes without risking a check-raise or losing to a slow-played marginal hand.

I hold J5o on the button (SB) with 100BB stacks. This is a very weak hand, generally falling into the folding range in standard Heads-Up charts, especially against an opponent who has already shown a willingness to 3-bet (Hand #2). While I have position, the hand plays poorly post-flop and lacks the equity to withstand aggression. Folding and forfeiting the small blind is the prudent, low-variance play early in the match.

I hold Ten-Nine suited on the button, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has great post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Fives (5d 5c). The opponent has raised to 300 from the Button. In heads-up play, a pocket pair is a strong hand, generally ahead of a random opening range. I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 600, requiring 33% equity), and 55 has significantly more equity than that against a typical wide button opening range. A 3-bet is possible but risks bloating the pot out of position with a hand that plays poorly if overcards hit. Calling is the standard and most prudent play to see a flop and potentially hit a set or navigate a low board.

I hold Pocket Fives on a J-4-6 board. I have second pair (or rather, a pocket pair falling between the 4 and 6, effectively beating the 4 but losing to the 6 and J). The board offers some connectivity (4-5-6 sequence), meaning a 3 or 7 on the turn gives me an open-ended straight draw. However, I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) accomplishes little, as better hands call and worse hands fold. I will check and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action.

On a board of Js 4s 6h, I am the pre-flop aggressor with T9dd. My opponent checked to me. While I haven't hit the board directly, I have position and a range advantage. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate to fold out weak hands and maintain initiative. I also have some backdoor straight potential.

In Heads-Up play, a pair of Fives on a J-4-6 board holds reasonable value against a wide Button opening range, which includes many unimproved overcards and flush draws. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 300 to win a pot of 900 requires 25% equity). Against a standard wide c-bet range, my hand has significantly more than 25% equity (often 40-50% against overcards and draws). Additionally, I have '4-5-6' straight connectivity, meaning any 3 or 7 on the turn gives me an open-ended straight draw, and a 5 gives me a set. Folding here would be exploitable. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold Pocket Fives on a board of Js-4s-6h-4c. I checked and called a bet on the flop. The turn card is a 4, pairing the board. This card is relatively safe, as it is unlikely to have improved the opponent's range significantly unless they hold a 4 (trips) or a full house, though it does not improve my hand either. My pair of 5s is currently ahead of unpaired overcards and flush draws but behind any Jack, 6, or higher pocket pair. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) would likely only fold out worse hands and isolate me against better ones. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to induce bluffs or pot control. I will check and evaluate if facing a bet.

On the turn board of Js-4s-6h-4c, my Ten-high has very little equity and no real draws. My flop continuation bet was called, which suggests the opponent has a piece of the board (likely a Jack, a pair, or a spade flush draw). The 4c on the turn is unlikely to have improved my range more than theirs. By checking back, I keep the pot manageable and see a free river card. I can then evaluate whether to bluff or fold on the river based on the final card and my opponent's action.

The river brings an Ace, which is a scare card for my specific hand (Pocket Fives). The board now reads J-4-6-4-A. My hand constitutes Two Pair (Fives and Fours) with an Ace kicker, but this is effectively a low bluff-catcher. The opponent checked back the turn, a line often taken with draw-heavy hands, marginal pairs, or Ace-high for showdown value. The River Ace likely improves his Ax holdings to a better Two Pair. Betting here has no value as worse hands (missed flush draws) will fold and better hands (any pair of 6s, Js, As, or 4s) will call. My best strategy is to check and hope for a cheap showdown against a missed draw, or fold if facing a bet.

The Ace of Hearts on the river is a significant scare card that hits my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. After checking the turn to control the pot and realize equity, my Ten-high now has zero showdown value. Since my opponent has checked twice (turn and river), they are likely capping their range at a weak Jack or a middle pair. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to represent the Ace or a strong Jack, putting maximum pressure on their marginal holdings and forcing a fold from better hands that cannot call a bet on this runout.

On the River, the board reads J-4-6-4-A. My hand is Pocket Fives, giving me two pair (Fives and Fours) with an Ace kicker. The opponent's line (Bet Flop, Check Turn, Bet River) on this runout is concerning. The Turn check indicates he likely didn't have a strong Jack or trips at that point, but the River Ace hits a significant portion of his range (Ax) that would check back the Turn for pot control or showdown value. While he could be bluffing with a missed spade flush draw, the Ace is a scary card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Furthermore, holding two Fives blocks some of the straight draw bluffs (like 7-5 or 5-3). Given I only beat a pure bluff and lose to any Ace, Jack, Six, or pocket pair above 55, and considering the opponent's demonstrated passivity in previous hands (e.g., Hand #7), this bet likely represents value. I cannot profitably call.

Playing Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, sitting on the Button (Small Blind) with K3s. This is a standard opening hand due to the King blocker and suited potential, combined with positional advantage post-flop. My opponent has shown aggression with 3-bets previously (Hand #2), so a min-raise to 200 is prudent. It exerts pressure to steal the blind while keeping the price cheap if I need to fold to a re-raise. Folding is too passive for K3s in this format.

J5o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, even against a min-raise. While the price to call is attractive (100 chips to see a 300 chip pot), the hand's post-flop playability is poor and it is frequently dominated. I choose to fold and maintain my chip lead rather than enter a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with J2o. This is a weak starting hand, but in heads-up play, any Jack has some potential. However, my opponent 'Pro' has shown some aggression in previous hands and J2o is near the bottom of my opening range. I could raise to steal the blinds, but given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold this specific garbage hand and wait for a better spot to exploit my opponent's tendencies. Folding J2o from the small blind is a standard conservative play to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if called.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) should typically open-raise a very wide range of hands (often top 80-90%) due to the positional advantage post-flop and the opportunity to steal the Big Blind. Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o) falls within the top ~55% of hands, making it a standard open. My opponent, Flash, has folded to my raises in 2 out of 3 instances (Hand #1 and #10) and only 3-bet once (Hand #2), indicating that a min-raise has significant immediate fold equity. Although I folded J5o earlier, Q4o holds slightly better high-card value. I will raise to 200 to steal the pot or play the hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with T2o. The opponent has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, Ten-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the range and lacks post-flop playability. Given that we are early in the tournament and I have a comfortable stack, there is no need to defend such a weak hand out of position.

I am on the button with King-Nine suited, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent has folded several times to pre-flop aggression, and I have a comfortable stack. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure, staying consistent with my previous raise sizing from this position.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh7h facing a 3x open raise (300) from the Button. In Heads-Up play, Q7s is a standard defense due to its playability and equity against a wide opening range. The price is 200 to call into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. Against a typical button opening range, Q7s has significantly more than 33% equity. Flash has shown a preference for this 3x sizing previously (Hands 5, 7, 9), so this doesn't necessarily indicate a premium hand. Folding here would be over-folding. A 3-bet is possible but calling is the standard low-variance play to realize equity.

On this Flop of 8d 4h 5d, I have Queen high with backdoor flush and straight possibilities (hearts and 6s). As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, the standard GTO approach is to check my entire range to the aggressor. The board texture connects reasonably well with a defensive calling range, but leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and unbalanced here. I will check to see how Flash proceeds. If they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, as I have two overcards and backdoor equity, but for now, checking is the correct move.

I have flopped a strong flush draw with two overcards (King and Nine). Since the opponent checked, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I still have significant equity to improve on the turn or river.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh7h facing a 300 chip continuation bet (half-pot) on a board of 8d 4h 5d. I need 25% equity to call. While I hold two overcards and have backdoor flush and straight possibilities (hearts and a gutshot if a 6 comes), my hand is currently just Queen-high. Being out of position makes realizing this equity difficult, especially if the opponent fires a second barrel on the turn. Standard GTO strategy generally folds unmade hands without strong immediate draws against half-pot bets on connected textures. I have many better hands in my range to defend with (pairs, flush draws, straight draws), so folding this marginal hand is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 7s 3c, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (73 offsuit). Although having position post-flop in heads-up play is advantageous, this hand has very little playability or equity against any reasonable calling range. I have folded similar weak hands (84o, J5o) previously. Preserving my stack is better than attempting a low-probability steal or playing a bloated pot with trash cards.

I am on the Button with 9h 5d, a relatively weak hand even for heads-up play. While I have position post-flop, the hand doesn't play well against a defending range from the Big Blind. Given my current lead in the tournament and the marginal nature of the hand, I will elect to fold and wait for a better spot to exploit the opponent's tendencies.

In a Heads-Up match, 8h 5d is a very weak hand (bottom 15% range). While Button opening ranges are wide, this hand has poor playability and connectivity. The opponent (Flash) has shown a willingness to defend their Big Blind (calling or 3-betting in previous hands), making an attempt to steal with this specific holding negative EV. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with pocket eights, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Given my stack size and the current tournament stage, I want to raise for value and to take control of the hand. My standard open from the button in previous hands has been to 300, and I will maintain that consistency here.

I hold 4c 3h in the Big Blind, which is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. The opponent, acting as the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300 (3bb). In Heads-Up play, defending the Big Blind is important, but 43o is at the very bottom of my range. Against a standard opening range, this hand has poor equity (roughly 30%) and very poor playability. Furthermore, the opponent's sizing of 3bb (rather than a min-raise) offers me worse pot odds (3:1) compared to a standard 2bb open, and I would be playing a bloated pot out of position with trash cards. Folding is the only profitable play here.

I am on the Button (SB) with Q4s, a hand that falls well within the standard opening range for heads-up play due to its flush potential and high card value. My opponent, Flash, has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop raises in several previous hands (Hands 1, 10, 12, 15), indicating reasonable fold equity. Raising to 200 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or play the post-flop in position with a suited hand. Folding is too passive for this holding, and limping forfeits the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. The pot is 300 and it costs me 100 more to call, giving me 3:1 odds. K3o is a marginal hand, but given the pot odds and the fact that we are heads-up, it is generally strong enough to defend against a standard button open, especially with a stack of over 100 big blinds. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

The flop is 5s-4s-2c. I have K3o, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing an Ace or a Six). However, the board is low and relatively connected, which often favors the Big Blind's range, but also hits many small pairs and connectors in the Button's opening range. Pro has checked to me (since the log shows the action is on me and the current street 'To Call' is 0). While I have a gutshot, my King-high also has some showdown value. I will check to see a free turn card and keep the pot small while I try to improve my hand.

I hold middle pair (4d) on a low, connected board of [5s 4s 2c]. The board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (containing pairs, straights, and draws). While my hand has showdown value, it is not strong enough to comfortably bet for value across multiple streets, and betting opens me up to a check-raise which would be difficult to defend against. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets. It also protects my checking range. I will check to see the turn.

The turn card is the Deuce of Spades, which pairs the board and completes a potential flush draw. Given that both Pro and I checked the flop, it is less likely that Pro has a very strong hand like an overpair or a set, as he probably would have bet those for protection or value. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing an Ace or a Six). My King-high still has some showdown value against his total air, and I want to see the river as cheaply as possible to realize my equity. I will check to see if Pro checks back or if I need to evaluate a bet from him.

The turn brings the [2s], putting three spades on the board and pairing the bottom card. This significantly changes the texture, completing possible flushes and giving trips to any 2x in the Big Blind's range. While I still have a pair of Fours (now two pair Fours and Deuces), my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher. Betting here yields little value as worse hands (unimproved high cards) will fold, and better hands (Fives, Trips, Flushes) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid being check-raised, and try to get to a cheap showdown with my marginal made hand.

The board has double-paired (5s-5h-2c-2s) with a 4. My best five-card hand is two pair, Fives and Deuces, with a King kicker (5-5-2-2-K). Given that both of us have checked through the flop and turn, it is unlikely that either of us has a strong hand like a full house or a flush. My King-high is a marginal showdown hand that beats any hand Queen-high or lower and ties with any other King-high. Since betting is unlikely to force an Ace-high to fold or get a worse hand to call, checking is the most logical play to reach showdown cheaply.

I hold Two Pair (Fives and Fours) with a Queen kicker on a double-paired board of [5s 4s 2c 2s 5h]. The board contains a possible flush and full house possibilities for any 2 or 5. My opponent has checked to me on all three streets. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like Ace-high or King-high, which would simply fold), while better hands (Trips, Full Houses, Flushes) would certainly call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against his weak range without risking more chips on a dangerous board.

I am on the button with KTo, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard play is to raise to gain initiative and build the pot. A 3x open (300 total) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 3h, a very weak unsuited hand. The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, offering me 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). Against a 3x opening range, 53o has poor equity (roughly 30-32%) and extremely poor playability out of position. While I need to defend my blind in heads-up play, this hand is at the very bottom of my range and is a standard fold against a 3x raise.

I hold Ac7s on the Button (SB), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, typically ranking in the top 30% of hands. My opponent, Flash, has shown a mix of folding to raises and calling, with one instance of a 3-bet. A7o is too strong to fold and strong enough to raise for value and protection, aiming to take down the blind immediately or play a pot in position with the initiative. A standard min-raise to 200 creates a good risk-reward ratio, putting pressure on the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, this hand has very poor playability out of position. I have previously folded similar hands like Jack-Five and Ten-Deuce to a 2x raise. Maintaining my chip lead and avoiding marginal spots with weak holdings is a sound strategy at this stage of the match.

I am holding Ace-Seven suited on the button. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Based on previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has been playing relatively conservatively, frequently folding to pre-flop raises or folding early post-flop. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain my aggressive strategy as the button.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 3c, a very weak hand. My opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. To call, I need to put in 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, giving me 33% pot odds. Although 93o has roughly 32-36% raw equity against a wide opening range, it suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds, especially while playing out of position. Defending 93o against a 3x raise is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

I hold Pocket Tens (Ts Tc) on the Button/SB. This is a premium hand in Heads-Up play. My standard opening strategy, as established in previous hands like Hand #2, #4, #10, and #20, has been to min-raise to 200. Maintaining this sizing disguises the strength of my hand while building the pot for value. If the opponent 3-bets, I am in a strong position to continue.

A standard raise to 300 (3x) is effective here to build the pot while keeping room to maneuver if he calls, though at this stack size, we are often looking to get it in if he moves all-in.

I am in the Big Blind with 14BB (1400 chips) facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My hand is Q3s. In Heads-Up play, suited Queens have sufficient equity to defend against a wide opening range. Pot odds are 200 to call into a pot that will be 600, giving me 33% odds, which my hand easily meets against a standard opening range. Shoving 14BB with Q3s is marginally profitable but high variance; calling allows me to see a flop. With an SPR of roughly 1.8 post-flop, I will play 'fit or fold' on the flop, committing if I hit top pair or a strong draw.

On the flop of [Jd 8c 6s], I hold Queen-high (Qh 3h) with no pair and no straight or flush draws. The opponent has made a continuation bet of 300 chips into a pot of 600. At 11.5 big blinds, I am in the push/fold territory. A7o is a strong hand heads-up (top ~29% of hands) and is well within the Nash Equilibrium shoving range for this stack depth. Min-raising carries the risk of being 3-bet shoved by the aggressive chip leader (as seen in Hands 82 and 56), which would put me in a tough spot or force a fold of a decent hand. Limping allows the opponent to realize equity for free. Shoving maximizes fold equity and prevents the opponent from leveraging their stack advantage to bluff me post-flop or pre-flop.

I started the hand with roughly 1200 chips and raised to 1150 pre-flop, committing nearly my entire stack. I have only 50 chips left. Flash has led out for 50 on the flop. The pot is 2350, and I need to call 50 to see the showdown. This offers me pot odds of 47:1, requiring roughly 2% equity to break even. With Ace-high (A7o), I have significantly more equity than 2% against any range, even if the opponent has hit the King. Folding for 50 chips into a pot of this size is mathematically impossible. I am pot-committed and must call.
